attrs.alt: Actions vs NotActions: effective permission calculation showing subtraction semantics and critical NotActions-is-not-deny note

content.text: Every Azure resource operation is identified by a string in the format `<provider>/<resourceType>/<operation>`. The `Actions` array in a role definition lists the control-plane (ARM) operations the role permits. Wildcards expand at evaluation time: `Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/*` matches every operation on that resource type, including ones added to the provider after the role was created.

Three standard operations exist on every resource type: `read`, `write`, and `delete`. Actions that do not fit CRUD use the `/action` suffix (e.g. `Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/start/action`). Sub-resources follow the parent type with a nested segment (e.g. `Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/extensions/read`).

# Azure operation string anatomy
# Format: <ProviderNamespace>/<ResourceType>[/<SubResourceType>]/<Operation>

# Examples:
# Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/read             # read VM properties
# Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/write            # create or update a VM
# Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/delete           # delete a VM
# Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/start/action     # invoke start action on VM
# Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/extensions/read  # read VM extension sub-resource

# Wildcard rules:
# Microsoft.Compute/*                  all Compute operations
# Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/*  all operations on the virtualMachines type
# */read                               all read operations across all providers

# Enumerate operations for a provider
Get-AzProviderOperation 'Microsoft.KeyVault/*' |
    Select-Object Operation, OperationName, IsDataAction |
    Sort-Object IsDataAction, Operation

# Distinguish control-plane (Actions) from data-plane (DataActions)
Get-AzProviderOperation 'Microsoft.Storage/*' |
    Where-Object { $_.IsDataAction -eq $true } |
    Select-Object Operation, OperationName |
    Sort-Object Operation

When building an Actions array, include not just the primary resource operations but also the auxiliary read operations that the Azure portal and CLI require to display context. A role that grants only `Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/start/action` will work programmatically, but users will see errors in the portal because they cannot list resource groups or read RBAC context.

# Role definition with explicit Actions (no wildcards)
# Scenario: allow reading and starting VMs, nothing else
{
  "Name": "VM Start Operator",
  "Actions": [
    "Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/read",
    "Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/instanceView/read",
    "Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/start/action",
    "Microsoft.Compute/locations/*/read",
    "Microsoft.Resources/subscriptions/resourceGroups/read",
    "Microsoft.Authorization/*/read",
    "Microsoft.Insights/alertRules/*",
    "Microsoft.Support/*"
  ],
  "NotActions": [],
  "DataActions": [],
  "NotDataActions": [],
  "AssignableScopes": [ "/subscriptions/SUBSCRIPTION-ID" ]
}

# Why include Microsoft.Resources/.../read and Microsoft.Authorization/*/read?
# Portal and CLI need these to enumerate resource groups and display RBAC context.
# Without them the assignment works but the portal shows access errors.

# Why include Microsoft.Insights/alertRules/* and Microsoft.Support/*?
# Azure portal requires alertRules/* for the Alerts blade.
# Support/* allows opening Azure support tickets — standard inclusion in operator roles.

Standard portal-support inclusions for operator roles: Microsoft.Resources/subscriptions/resourceGroups/read, Microsoft.Authorization/*/read, Microsoft.Insights/alertRules/*, Microsoft.Support/*. Add these to every role intended for human operators.

NotActions subtracts operations from the set established by Actions. It is most useful when Actions contains a wildcard that covers more than intended. Rather than enumerating every allowed operation, grant the wildcard and exclude the dangerous ones via NotActions.

The most important semantic to understand: NotActions is NOT a deny. It only reduces the effective permissions within the single role definition that declares it. If the same principal holds another role that explicitly grants the operation, they can still perform it.

# Demonstrate that NotActions is NOT a deny

# Role A: grants delete
# Actions: ['Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/*']
# NotActions: []

# Role B: wildcards VMs but excludes delete in NotActions
# Actions: ['Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/*']
# NotActions: ['Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/delete']

# Assign BOTH roles to the same principal at the same scope:
$principalId = 'USER-OBJECT-ID'
$scope = '/subscriptions/SUB/resourceGroups/rg-compute'

New-AzRoleAssignment -ObjectId $principalId -RoleDefinitionName 'Role A' -Scope $scope
New-AzRoleAssignment -ObjectId $principalId -RoleDefinitionName 'Role B' -Scope $scope

# Result: the principal CAN delete VMs.
# Role A grants delete explicitly. Role B's NotActions only subtracts from Role B's
# effective set — it does not affect Role A's grant.
# Azure evaluates: Union(all Allow grants) minus Deny Assignments
# NotActions are applied per-role during the Union step, not after.

# To block delete even when another role grants it: use a Deny Assignment.
# Deny Assignments are created via Azure Blueprints or Azure Policy (managed deny).
Yes — any Allow from any role wins

When a principal makes a request, Azure RBAC evaluates permissions in a defined order. First, it collects all role assignments that apply to the principal at the target scope (including inherited assignments from parent scopes). For each role, it computes the role-effective set as Actions minus NotActions. It then takes the union of all role-effective sets to form the total allowed set. Finally, it subtracts any Deny Assignments. The request is permitted only if the target operation is in the remaining set.

# How Azure evaluates effective permissions

# Step 1: collect all role assignments for the principal at the requested scope
#         (includes assignments at parent scopes due to inheritance)
Get-AzRoleAssignment -ObjectId 'USER-OBJECT-ID' -Scope '/subscriptions/SUB'

# Step 2: for each role, compute role-effective = Actions - NotActions
# Step 3: Union of all role-effective sets = total allowed actions
# Step 4: subtract any Deny Assignments
# Step 5: check: requested action in result?

# PowerShell: get effective actions for a specific principal
# (Use the What If API for a real operation check)

# Simulate what a principal can do (preview feature, requires permissions)
Invoke-AzRestMethod -Method POST \`
    -Path '/subscriptions/SUB/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/permissions?api-version=2022-04-01' \`
    -Payload (@{ resourceId = '/subscriptions/SUB/resourceGroups/rg/providers/Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/my-vm' } | ConvertTo-Json)

# Practical alternative: use Test-AzRoleAssignmentPermission (Az.Resources)
# or the IAM blade 'Check access' in the Azure portal to verify effective permissions

Three patterns cover most custom role use cases: a wildcard with dangerous operations removed via NotActions, an explicit allowlist with no wildcards, and a cloned built-in role trimmed with NotActions. Each has different tradeoffs between security posture and maintenance burden.

# Pattern 1: Wildcard with NotActions subtraction (maintenance-friendly)
# Grants everything in a namespace except dangerous destructive operations
{
  "Actions": [ "Microsoft.Compute/*" ],
  "NotActions": [
    "Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/delete",
    "Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachineScaleSets/delete",
    "Microsoft.Compute/disks/delete"
  ]
}

# Pattern 2: Explicit allowlist (security-first, more maintenance)
# New operations added to the provider are NOT included automatically
{
  "Actions": [
    "Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/read",
    "Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/write",
    "Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/start/action",
    "Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/restart/action"
  ],
  "NotActions": []
}

# Pattern 3: Clone built-in and trim (quick wins)
$base = Get-AzRoleDefinition -Name 'Contributor'
$trimmed = [Microsoft.Azure.Commands.Resources.Models.Authorization.PSRoleDefinition]::new()
$trimmed.Name = 'Contributor No-RBAC'
$trimmed.Description = 'Contributor without ability to manage role assignments'
$trimmed.IsCustom = $true
$trimmed.Actions = $base.Actions  # includes wildcard *
$trimmed.NotActions = @(
    'Microsoft.Authorization/roleAssignments/write'
    'Microsoft.Authorization/roleAssignments/delete'
    'Microsoft.Authorization/roleDefinitions/write'
    'Microsoft.Authorization/roleDefinitions/delete'
)
$trimmed.AssignableScopes = @('/subscriptions/SUBSCRIPTION-ID')
New-AzRoleDefinition -Role $trimmed

Over time, custom role definitions accumulate wildcards, duplicate entries, and NotActions exclusions that were meant to be hard blocks but are undermined by other roles. Regular audits identify roles with overly broad wildcards or roles whose NotActions exclusions are rendered ineffective by other assignments.

Wildcards are convenient but require careful analysis. When you write `Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/*`, you grant every current and future operation on that resource type — including dangerous ones like `runCommand/action` (execute arbitrary code on the VM) and `extensions/write` (install arbitrary software). Before using a wildcard, enumerate what it expands to and add the dangerous operations to NotActions.

# Wildcard risk analysis — what does Microsoft.Compute/* actually include?

# Enumerate all operations covered by a wildcard
$allComputeOps = Get-AzProviderOperation 'Microsoft.Compute/*'
Write-Host "Total Compute operations: $($allComputeOps.Count)"

# Find dangerous operations that a wildcard would silently include
$dangerous = $allComputeOps | Where-Object {
    $_.Operation -match 'delete|runCommand|extensions/write|secrets/list'
}
$dangerous | Select-Object Operation, OperationName | Sort-Object Operation

# runCommand is especially dangerous: allows executing arbitrary code on VMs
# Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/runCommand/action
# If your wildcard role should not allow this, add it to NotActions:
$role.NotActions = @(
    'Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/delete'
    'Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/runCommand/action'  # execute code on VM
    'Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/extensions/write'   # install VM extensions
    'Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/extensions/delete'
    'Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachineScaleSets/delete'
)

# Check if a specific operation is included in an existing role's effective set
Get-AzRoleDefinition -Name 'Virtual Machine Operator' | ForEach-Object {
    $effective = Get-AzProviderOperation 'Microsoft.Compute/*' |
        Where-Object { $_ -in $_.Actions } |
        Where-Object { $_ -notin $_.NotActions }
    'Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/runCommand/action' -in $effective
}
Execute any command on the OS as SYSTEM/root
Add to NotActions unless explicitly needed
Add to NotActions for operator roles
Review if data exfil is a concern
Add to NotActions for read-only audit roles
Multiple Role Assignments and NotActions Interaction

The most common misconception about NotActions is that it creates a security boundary. It does not. Understanding how Azure evaluates multiple role assignments side-by-side is essential for designing correct access models.

# Demonstrate how multiple role assignments interact with NotActions

# Create two roles: one broad, one narrow with NotActions
$broadRole = @{
    Name = 'Storage Full Access'
    Actions = @('Microsoft.Storage/*')
    NotActions = @()
    AssignableScopes = @('/subscriptions/SUB')
}

$narrowRole = @{
    Name = 'Storage No Delete'
    Actions = @('Microsoft.Storage/*')
    NotActions = @('Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/delete')
    AssignableScopes = @('/subscriptions/SUB')
}

# Assign BOTH to the same principal
New-AzRoleAssignment -ObjectId $userId -RoleDefinitionName 'Storage Full Access' -Scope $scope
New-AzRoleAssignment -ObjectId $userId -RoleDefinitionName 'Storage No Delete' -Scope $scope

# The user CAN delete storage accounts.
# 'Storage Full Access' grants delete. 'Storage No Delete' NotActions cannot remove it.

# If you only assign 'Storage No Delete' (no other storage role):
# The user CANNOT delete storage accounts — NotActions works within a single role.

# Lesson: NotActions is a role-trimming tool, not a security boundary.
# Security boundaries require Deny Assignments.

Design rule: if you need to guarantee that an operation is blocked regardless of what other roles a user holds, use a Deny Assignment — not NotActions. NotActions is a convenience for trimming within a single role.

When auditing a role hierarchy or debugging an access problem, comparing the effective action sets of two roles helps identify gaps and overlaps. The function below expands wildcards and subtracts NotActions to produce a concrete list of permitted operations.

Actions and NotActions are the two core fields that define what a custom role can do at the ARM control plane. Actions lists allowed operations (with optional wildcards); NotActions subtracts from that set within the role. The critical invariant: NotActions is never a deny — it only refines the role's own permission set. To enforce a hard block, use a Deny Assignment.
Operation strings follow <provider>/<type>/<operation>; wildcards expand at evaluation time.
Include auxiliary portal-support operations (resourceGroups/read, Authorization/*/read, alertRules/*, Support/*) in human-facing roles.
Wildcard + NotActions reduces maintenance burden at the cost of auto-including new operations.
Explicit allowlists are more secure but require manual updates when providers add new operations.
NotActions within role B does NOT block the same operation granted by role A on the same principal.
Use Deny Assignments (via Blueprints or Policy) to create blocks that cannot be overridden by Allow grants.
